nodes.bio: Chicago. Recorded thousands at Electrical Audio. Refused royalties. 'What you hear is what was played.'
Chicago. Albini's Shellac bandmate. Prolific at Chicago Mastering Service and beyond.
Minneapolis/Chicago. Slint's Spiderland, Wilco, Beck, Son Volt, Polvo, Superchunk.
Chicago. Tortoise drummer. Soma Electronic Music Studios. Post-rock architect.
Chicago experimentalist. Gastr del Sol, Wilco YHF, Stereolab, Smog, Sonic Youth.
Shimmy-Disc founder. Galaxie 500, Low, Will Oldham, Daniel Johnston.
Cassadaga, NY. Tarbox Road Studios. Mercury Rev, Flaming Lips, Mogwai.
Chicago. Idful Studios. Liz Phair, Smashing Pumpkins, Tortoise.
London. Radiohead's 'sixth member.' OK Computer, Kid A, Amnesiac.
NYC. Dinosaur Jr, Sonic Youth, Jawbox, Jawbreaker, Breeders.
Seattle. Reciprocal Studios. Nirvana, Soundgarden, Mudhoney. Grunge godfather.
Arlington, VA. Inner Ear Studios. Fugazi, Minor Threat, Dischord Records.
New Orleans/Canada. U2, Dylan, Emmylou Harris. Atmospheric perfectionist.
Madison, WI. Smart Studios. Nirvana, Smashing Pumpkins, Garbage.
Bristol, UK. Portishead. Trip-hop / sample-based production.
London. Abbey Road veteran. Radiohead, Muse, Stone Roses, Spiritualized.
Albini's temple of recording. Two buildings, live rooms, no autotune.
Bob Weston's home base for mastering and recording.
John McEntire's studio. Named after the drug in Brave New World.
Dave Fridmann's rural studio in upstate New York.
Butch Vig and Steve Marker's studio, ground zero for 90s alt-rock.
Don Zientara's basement studio, Dischord Records nerve center.
Chris Hanzsek and Jack Endino's grunge birthplace.
The most famous studio in the world. John Leckie worked here early.
Post-rock. Young Team (1997), Come On Die Young (1999), Rock Action (2001).
Slowcore pioneers. Things We Lost In The Fire (2001), produced by Albini.
Albini + Weston + Trainer. At Action Park (1994), Terraform (1998), 1000 Hurts (2000).
Being There (1996), Summerteeth (1999), Yankee Hotel Foxtrot (2002).
Post-rock instrumentalists. TNT (1998), Standards (2001).
Krautrock-pop hybrid. Dots and Loops (1997), Cobra and Phases (1999).
Soft Bulletin (1999), Yoshimi (2002). Fridmann's orchestral psychedelia.
Deserter's Songs (1998). Chamber-pop masterpiece via Fridmann.
OK Computer (1997), Kid A (2000), Amnesiac (2001).
Where You Been (1993), Hand It Over (1997). J Mascis + Lou Barlow.
A Thousand Leaves (1998), NYC Ghosts & Flowers (2000), Murray Street (2002).
Nevermind (1991), In Utero (1993). Endino / Vig / Albini.
Siamese Dream (1993), Mellon Collie (1995), Adore (1998).
Dummy (1994), Portishead (1997), Roseland NYC (1998). Trip-hop pioneers.
Red Medicine (1995), End Hits (1998), The Argument (2001). Dischord.
One Foot in the Grave (1994), Odelay (1996), Sea Change (2002).
Indie rock institution. Merge Records founders. Paulson connections.
Math/pitchfork rock. Exploded Drawing (1996), Shapes (1997).
Post-rock / jazz-pop. Oui (2000), One Bedroom (2003). McEntire.
Lo-fi indie. Bakesale (1994), Harmacy (1996). Weston connections.
Mark Linkous. Good Morning Spider (1998), It's A Wonderful Life (2001).
Palace Brothers, Bonnie 'Prince' Billy. I See a Darkness (1999).
Exile in Guyville (1993), Whitechocolatespaceegg (1998). Brad Wood.
Showbiz (1999), Origin of Symmetry (2001). John Leckie.
On Fire (1989), This Is Our Music (1990). Kramer. Pre-era but foundational.
Lo-fi folk legend. Kramer-produced early releases on Shimmy-Disc.
Post-punk. Weston remastered their catalog.
Post-rock / krautrock. McEntire connections.
Vee Vee (1995), All the Nation's Airports (1996). Weston.
Garbage (1995), Version 2.0 (1998). Butch Vig's band.
Bandwagonesque (1991), Songs from Northern Britain (1997). McEntire.
Badmotorfinger (1991), Superunknown (1994). Endino.
Ladies and Gentlemen (1997). John Leckie engineer.
For Your Own Special Sweetheart (1994). John Agnello.
Dear You (1995). John Agnello produced.
Last Splash (1993), Title TK (2002). John Agnello.
Trace (1995), Wide Swing Tremolo (1998). Alt-country via Brian Paulson.
Bill Callahan. Knock Knock (1999). Drag City lo-fi.
Winston-Salem NC. Architect of R.E.M.'s early jangle; produced Chronic Town/Murmur/Reckoning. Let's Active frontman; first 'studio' was his parents' garage.
Lancaster SC. Co-produced R.E.M.'s first two LPs with Easter; power-pop go-to (Smithereens). Staff engineer at Reflection Sound, Charlotte.
Chapel Hill NC. dB's co-founder; produces alt-country to riot-grrrl. Played with Alex Chilton; briefly Yo La Tengo's bassist.
Nashville TN. Lambchop's 'fifth head,' warm vintage-tape post-country sound. Once quit music to cook.
Skyline-trained, Nashville-based. Produced Yo La Tengo's classic run; also Lou Reed, Sleater-Kinney.
Memphis-trained, Texas-based. Raw, room-forward records. Grammy for engineering Loretta Lynn's Van Lear Rose.
Boston. MIT EE turned producer; Hüsker Dü's live soundman → Sugar/Goo Goo Dolls. Built pedals for Mission of Burma.
Ellensburg→Seattle. Connective tissue of the early PNW underground; Screaming Trees, Beat Happening, Low.
Seattle. Sub Pop's first-call producer/mixer; the Built to Spill trilogy → Fleet Foxes.
Seattle→Portland. Death Cab guitarist/producer; Transatlanticism, early Decemberists.
Olympia WA. K Records founder, Beat Happening frontman; built Dub Narcotic to record Olympia on lo-fi terms.
LA→NYC. The mix engineer who defined grunge/alt-rock punch (Nevermind); full production of Buckley's Grace.
London. Shoegaze sonic architect; Trident-trained; longtime Flood/Reznor collaborator.
London. Producer behind Depeche Mode/U2's reinvention/NIN/PJ Harvey. Born Mark Ellis.
London (Irish-American). MBV leader; 'glide guitar' pioneer; Loveless famously used ~25 studios.
Bristol. Jazz-schooled Portishead guitarist/producer; key to the trip-hop sound; vast vintage-gear collection.
Glasgow. Belle & Sebastian/Mogwai/Delgados producer-engineer. Now co-owns Castle of Doom.
English-born Chicago engineer (1953–2010). Defined the gritty 'Chicago sound'; a template for the engineer-not-producer ethos Albini made famous.
Chicago. Electrical Audio engineer/tech; heavy/post-metal sessions (Russian Circles, Pelican).
Chicago. Idful-era engineer + Tortoise live sound; later electronic producer in Australia.
Washington DC. Fugazi/Rites of Spring guitarist; producer of arty post-punk (Blonde Redhead).
Washington DC. Dischord co-founder; near-default recordist for Dischord-orbit bands, always alongside Don Zientara.
Denver/Athens. Elephant 6 co-founder, Apples in Stereo frontman; produced Neutral Milk Hotel. Compression-as-distortion technique.
Livonia MI. His Name Is Alive auteur; recorded hundreds of others; now masters at Third Man.
Benton Harbor MI. Runs Key Club Recording with Jessica Ruffins; analog garage-rock + builds their own gear.
Brooklyn NY (French-born). Defining 2000s–10s Brooklyn recordist (Animal Collective, Deerhunter, War on Drugs).
Manhattan. No Wave drummer (ex-Glenn Branca) turned engineer; 200+ records; early Sonic Youth/Dinosaur Jr/Helmet.
Gowanus, Brooklyn. Founded BC Studio in 1981 with seed money from Brian Eno + Bill Laswell; four decades of NYC noise.
Mitch Easter's purpose-built room (1999); jangle/indie destination.
Mark Nevers' warm vintage-tape room off Music Row; Lambchop HQ.
Chris Stamey's studio.
Stuart Sikes' room (2006); raw and room-forward.
Garage/indie destination; early White Stripes, Pavement, Sonic Youth.
Workhorse PNW indie room; home to Fisk and Ek.
Walla's triangular century-old building (2000–05); Death Cab/Decemberists.
Hand-carved stone facility, legendary drum room; Nirvana's final session, Foo Fighters' debut.
Calvin Johnson's lo-fi basement room; the K Records sound.
Robert Schneider's home studio; In the Aeroplane Over the Sea.
Analog room + pro-audio gear shop, run by Skibbe & Ruffins.
Vernhes' 2000s–10s Brooklyn indie hub.
Wharton Tiers' basement room (~1981–2007); 200+ underground records.
Founded 1981 with Eno/Laswell money; NYC noise/no-wave cornerstone.
Iain Burgess + Peter Deimel's room after he left Chicago (1993).
Doogan + Mogwai's studio.
Chronic Town (1982), Murmur (1983), Reckoning (1984). Easter/Dixon jangle.
Scott Miller's paisley power-pop. Real Nighttime (1985), The Big Shot Chronicles (1986).
Slanted and Enchanted (1992), Brighten the Corners (1997).
Mary Timony. The Magic City (1997). Mitch Easter.
Power-pop. Especially for You (1986), Green Thoughts (1988). Don Dixon.
Ryan Adams. Faithless Street (1995). Chris Stamey.
Kathleen Hanna's electro-feminist project. Le Tigre (1999).
Queercore. Population: 1975 (1999).
A Man Under the Influence (2001). Chris Stamey.
Post-country collective. What Another Man Spills (1998), Nixon (2000). Mark Nevers.
David Berman. American Water (1998), Bright Flight (2001).
Country legend. Charlie Louvin (2007). Mark Nevers.
Painful (1993), I Can Hear the Heart Beating as One (1997). Roger Moutenot.
This Fire (1996). Roger Moutenot.
Emo. Nothing Feels Good (1997). Stuart Sikes engineer.
Blake Schwarzenbach post-Jawbreaker. Orange Rhyming Dictionary (1998).
White Blood Cells (2001). Stuart Sikes engineer.
Van Lear Rose (2004), produced by Jack White, engineered by Sikes.
Chan Marshall. The Greatest (2006). Stuart Sikes.
Bob Mould post-Hüsker Dü. Copper Blue (1992). Lou Giordano.
A Boy Named Goo (1995). Lou Giordano.
Replacements frontman solo. Eventually (1996). Lou Giordano.
Hot-jazz revival. The Inevitable (1995). Brian Paulson.
Clairvoyance (1986), Buzz Factory (1989). Steve Fisk.
Twee/lo-fi. Jamboree (1988), You Turn Me On (1992). Calvin Johnson / Steve Fisk.
Doug Martsch. Perfect from Now On (1997), Keep It Like a Secret (1999). Phil Ek.
The Lonesome Crowded West (1997), The Moon & Antarctica (2000). Phil Ek.
Chutes Too Narrow (2003). Phil Ek.
Everything All the Time (2005). Phil Ek.
Chamber-folk. Fleet Foxes (2008). Phil Ek.
Transatlanticism (2003), Plans (2005). Chris Walla.
Picaresque (2005), The Crane Wife (2006). Chris Walla.
The Con (2007). Chris Walla.
The Weight Is a Gift (2005). Chris Walla.
Grace (1994). Andy Wallace, full production.
Automatic (1989). Alan Moulder.
Isn't Anything (1988), Loveless (1991). Kevin Shields / Alan Moulder.
Shoegaze. Mezcal Head (1993). Alan Moulder.
Pretty Hate Machine (1989), The Downward Spiral (1994). Flood / Moulder.
Violator (1990). Flood.
The Joshua Tree (1987), Achtung Baby (1991). Lanois / Flood.
To Bring You My Love (1995). Flood.
XTRMNTR (2000). Kevin Shields co-produced.
Out of Season (2002). Adrian Utley.
The Boy with the Arab Strap (1998). Tony Doogan.
Albini's first band. Atomizer (1986). Iain Burgess engineered.
Throb Throb (1985), All Rise (1986). Iain Burgess.
Strong Reaction (1991). Iain Burgess.
Instrumental post-metal. Enter (2006), Geneva (2009). Greg Norman.
Instrumental post-metal. The Fire in Our Throats Will Beckon the Thaw (2005). Greg Norman.
Melody of Certain Damaged Lemons (2000), Misery Is a Butterfly (2004). Guy Picciotto.
Gospel-yeh-yeh. Destination: Love (1996). Guy Picciotto.
Dischord. No Kill No Beep Beep (2000). MacKaye/Zientara.
Dischord. Rainbows from Atoms (1993). MacKaye/Zientara.
On Avery Island (1996), In the Aeroplane Over the Sea (1998). Robert Schneider.
Fun Trick Noisemaker (1995). Robert Schneider.
4AD dream-pop. Mouth by Mouth (1993). Warren Defever.
Midnight Boom (2008). Skibbe & Ruffins at Key Club.
Strawberry Jam (2007). Nicolas Vernhes.
Microcastle (2008). Nicolas Vernhes.
Lost in the Dream (2014). Nicolas Vernhes.
Noise/alt-metal. Strap It On (1990), Meantime (1992). Wharton Tiers.

nodes.id: Don Dixon, Bland Simpson & Jim Wann
The Presidents of the United States of America

nodes.bio: Slint / Tortoise / Aerial M guitarist who records his own work. Papa M's Whatever, Mortal (2001) is credited 'produced by no one in particular.'
David Pajo's project. Whatever, Mortal (2001, Drag City) — largely self-recorded, with Will Oldham guesting, mastered by John Golden.
